questions.text: “Law of independent assortment” states:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: That each pair of alleles assort independently of other pairs of alleles during gamete formation
options.explanation: This statement accurately represents the Law of Independent Assortment. It means that genes located on different chromosomes are inherited independently of each other during gamete formation.

options.text: That alleles of each pair of contrasting trait have unequal probability to assort with the alleles of other pair
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. The Law of Independent Assortment states that alleles segregate independently, regardless of the alleles at other gene loci. The probability of assorting is equal for all alleles.

options.text: That the two coexisting alleles for each trait segregate (separate) from each other at meiosis so that each gamete receives only one of the two alleles
options.explanation: This statement describes the Law of Segregation, not the Law of Independent Assortment. The Law of Segregation states that each individual has two alleles for each gene, and these alleles segregate during the formation of gametes.

options.text: That pertain to inheritance of single trait (monohybrid cross)
options.explanation: The Law of Independent Assortment is not specific to a single trait but applies to genes located on different chromosomes. The term "monohybrid cross" is more closely associated with the Law of Segregation.

questions.explanation: Mendel's law of independent assortment states that the alleles of two (or more) different genes get sorted into gametes independently of one another. In other words, the allele a gamete receives for one gene does not influence the allele received for another gene.

questions.text: A certain road accident patient with unknown blood group needs immediate blood transfusion. His one doctor friend at once offers his blood What was the blood group of the donor?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Blood group B individuals have B antigens on their red blood cells. While they can donate blood to individuals with blood groups B and AB, they cannot be considered universal donors.
Blood group AB individuals are considered universal recipients, as they can receive blood from any other blood group. However, they are not universal donors.

Blood group O individuals are considered universal donors because their red blood cells lack A or B antigens on their surfaces. Therefore, individuals with blood group O can donate blood to individuals with any other blood group without causing an adverse immune response due to antigen incompatibility.
Blood group A individuals have A antigens on their red blood cells. They can donate blood to individuals with blood groups A and AB, but they are not universal donors.

questions.explanation: Blood group O individuals are considered universal donors because their red blood cells lack A or B antigens on their surfaces. Therefore, individuals with blood group O can donate blood to individuals with any other blood group without causing an adverse immune response due to antigen incompatibility.

questions.text: In our society women are blamed for producing female children. Choose the correct answer for the sex ­determination in humans.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Due to a condition like aspermia in the man
options.explanation: Aspermia, the absence of sperm, can lead to infertility but has no impact on determining the sex of any offspring that are conceived.

options.text: Due to the genetic makeup of the particular sperm that fertilizes the egg
options.explanation: The sperm determines the sex of the offspring. If it carries an X chromosome, the resulting child is female (XX); if it carries a Y chromosome, the child is male (XY).

options.text: Due to the genetic makeup of the egg
options.explanation: All eggs from females contain an X chromosome, so they do not influence the sex of the offspring.

options.text: Due to a defect in the woman
options.explanation: Defects in a woman do not affect the genetic contribution of the egg or determine the sex of the offspring.

questions.explanation: In humans, sex determination is based on the combination of sex chromosomes at fertilization. A female is XX, and a male is XY. The egg always contributes an X chromosome, while the sperm can contribute either an X or a Y chromosome, resulting in female (XX) or male (XY) offspring. Therefore, the sex of the child is determined by the genetic makeup of the sperm, not the egg. Aspermia and defects in women do not impact this genetic determination process.

questions.text: The number of linkage groups in humans is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A is incorrect since it states 1/21 which is wrong.

B is correct since the number of linkage groups in humans is equal to the haploid number of chromosomes. The linkage groups refer to all the genes on a single chromosome. These groups are inherited together in an individual. The chromosomes in humans are 46 and their haploid number is 23. So linkage groups are also 23.
C is incorrect since it states 1/23 which is wrong.
D is incorrect since it states 1/24 which is wrong.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct since the number of linkage groups in humans is equal to the haploid number of chromosomes. The linkage groups refer to all the genes on a single chromosome. These groups are inherited together in an individual. The chromosomes in humans are 46 and their haploid number is 23. So linkage groups are also 23. Option A is incorrect since it states 1/21 which is wrong. Option C is incorrect since it states 1/23 which is wrong. Option D is incorrect since it states 1/24 which is wrong.

questions.text: Your neighbour has a flower garden in which there are red flowers and white flowers. These flowers are diploid organisms, and the flower colour is an autosomal trait. The gene for red flowers (R) is dominant, while the gene for white flowers (r) is recessive. Which of the following could be the genotype of a red flower?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If there is only the Rr allele, the flower will be red instead of white.
If the alleles are Rr and RR, both will produce red flowers. In case, if no 'R' is present and 'rr' are present, the flower will be white instead of red.
If alleles are 'rr' the flower will be white instead of red.

A red flower must have an 'R' allele which conveys the red phenotype. Since the red allele is dominant, the flower remains as long as red as one of the copies of this allele is present. Thus, both the 'Rr' and 'RR' will produce red flowers. In case, if no 'R' allele is present and 'rr' alleles are present, the flower will be white instead of red.

questions.explanation: Option A : If there is only the Rr allele, the flower will be red instead of white. Option B : If the alleles are Rr and RR, both will produce red flowers. Incase, if no 'R' is present and 'rr' are present, the flower will be white instead of red. Option C : If alleles are 'rr' the flower will be white instead of red. Option : D A red flower must have an 'R' allele which conveys the red phenotype. Since the red allele is dominant, the flower remains as long as red as one of the copies of this allele is present. Thus, both the 'Rr' and 'RR' will produce red flowers. In case, if no 'R' allele is present and 'rr' alleles are present, the flower will be white instead of red.

questions.text: Inheritance in man is traced by which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) Mathematical method: The term "mathematical method" is quite broad and can encompass various mathematical approaches used in genetics. However, when it comes to tracing inheritance in humans, specific mathematical tools and models are often employed to analyze and interpret genetic data. For example, probability calculations, Punnett squares, and statistical analyses are commonly used in genetics to predict inheritance patterns and genetic outcomes. While mathematics is an essential tool in genetic studies, it is not the primary method used for tracing inheritance in humans.

b) Pedigree method: This is the correct answer. The pedigree method is a systematic approach used to trace inheritance in humans and other organisms. A pedigree is a graphical representation of a family tree that shows the genetic relationships between family members and the occurrence of specific traits or diseases within the family. By analyzing the patterns of inheritance observed in the pedigree, geneticists can determine whether a trait is inherited in a dominant, recessive, or other pattern. Pedigree analysis is crucial in understanding the inheritance of genetic diseases and traits in human populations.

c) Statistical method: Statistical methods are used in genetics to analyze and interpret large datasets, but they are not the primary method for tracing inheritance in humans. Instead, statistical tools and techniques are often used in conjunction with the pedigree method to draw conclusions from genetic data and make predictions about inheritance patterns.

d) Genetic method: The term "genetic method" is quite broad and can refer to a variety of techniques used in genetic research. While genetics, in general, is the scientific field concerned with the study of genes and inheritance, there is no single "genetic method" that is used to trace inheritance in humans. Instead, various genetic approaches, including pedigree analysis, DNA sequencing, and molecular genetic techniques, are employed in the study of inheritance.

questions.explanation: A pedigree is a genetic representation of a family tree that diagrams the inheritance of a trait or disease through several generations. The pedigree shows the relationships between family members and indicates which individuals express or silently carry the trait in question.

questions.text: Mendel's law of independent assortment was given in:
questions.subject: Biology

This is not correct. Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment was given in 1865

questions.explanation: Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment was given in 1865 by Gregor Mendel through his experiments on pea plants.

questions.text: Sex linkage was discovered by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stevens was interested in the process of sex determination. While studying the mealworm, she found that the males made reproductive cells with both X and Y chromosomes whereas the females made only those with X.
T.H. Morgan conducted his famous experiments on Drosophila melanogaster and discovered the phenomenon of sex linkage. He studied reciprocal crosses between red-eyed and white-eyed fruit flies

Walter Stanborough Sutton was an American geneticist and biologist whose most significant contribution to present-day biology was his theory that the Mendelian laws of inheritance could be applied to chromosomes at the cellular level of living organisms. This is now known as the Boveri–Sutton chromosome theory.

questions.text: Which one of the following is Multiple allelic characters?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Colour of flower in pea plant
options.explanation: It is the incorrect option. Colour of flower in pea plant: Flower color in pea plants is typically determined by a single gene with two alleles (e.g., red and white), demonstrating simple Mendelian inheritance, not multiple alleles.

options.text: Blood group of the human being
options.explanation: Multiple alleles are the alternative forms of the same gene so they influence the same trait. An excellent example of multiple allele inheritance is human blood type. Blood type exists as four possible phenotypes: A, B, AB, & O. There are 3 alleles for the gene that determines blood type.

options.text: Shape of seed in pea plant
options.explanation: It is the incorrect option. Shape of seed in pea plant: Seed shape in pea plants, like flower color, is governed by a single gene with two alleles (e.g., round and wrinkled), again following Mendelian inheritance.

options.text: Length of stem in pea plant
options.explanation: It is the incorrect option.Length of stem in pea plant: Stem length is also usually controlled by a single gene with two alleles (e.g., tall and dwarf), not multiple alleles.

questions.explanation: Multiple alleles are the alternative forms of the same gene so they influence the same trait. An excellent example of multiple allele inheritance is human blood type. Blood type exists as four possible phenotypes: A, B, AB, & O. There are 3 alleles for the gene that determines blood type.

questions.text: A monohybrid cross yielded a 3:1 ratio in F2. What could be the mode of inheritance?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 3:1 in F2 generation is obtained when studying segregation. Hence, this option is correct. As the ratio is 3:1, it means there are only two traits under observation: the two possibilities for a gene (alleles). We study alleles for one trait in Mendel’s first law, also called the law of segregation. It states that during gamete formation, the two alleles for a gene segregate or separate, and each gamete takes only one allele. When two gametes join to form a zygote, the two alleles combine again.
In the F2 generation, while studying independent assortment, the ratio is 9:3:3:1, which contradicts the given ratio. Hence, this option cannot be correct.

options.text: Both Options A and B are correct
options.explanation: As only option A is correct, this option is incorrect.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: As option A is correct, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: As the ratio is 3:1, it means there are only two traits under observation: the two possibilities for a gene (alleles). We study alleles for one trait in Mendel’s first law, also called the law of segregation. It states that during gamete formation, the two alleles for a gene segregate or separate, and each gamete takes only one allele. When two gametes join to form a zygote, the two alleles combine again.

questions.text: If both the alleles are the same with respect to genes, then they are called?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Heterozygous refers to having different alleles for a particular trait.

A unicellular organism, also known as a single-celled organism, is an organism that consists of a single cell, unlike a multicellular organism that consists of multiple cells. Organisms fall into two general categories: prokaryotic organisms and eukaryotic organisms

If both alleles for a particular gene are the same, the individual is said to be "homozygous" for that gene. Homozygous means that both copies of the gene (one inherited from each parent) are identical. Homozygosity can occur with either two dominant alleles (homozygous dominant) or two recessive alleles (homozygous recessive). There are two main types of homozygous genotypes: 1. Homozygous Dominant (BB): In this case, both alleles are the dominant form of the gene (represented by "B" in this example). The individual will express the dominant trait because the dominant allele masks the effect of the recessive allele. 2. Homozygous Recessive (bb): Here, both alleles are the recessive form of the gene (represented by "b" in this example). The individual will express the recessive trait because there is no dominant allele to mask its expression. In both cases, the genotype is homozygous because both alleles are the same (either both dominant or both recessive). Homozygosity plays a crucial role in determining the phenotype (observable characteristics) of an individual for a specific trait controlled by that gene.
This is incorrect.

questions.explanation: If both of the two gametes (sex cells) that fuse during fertilization carry the same form of the gene for a specific trait, the organism is said to be 'homozygous' for that trait. 'A' is not an answer because heterozygous refers to having inherited different forms of a particular gene from each parent. 'B' is not the answer because the term unicellular refers to the state of a cell.

questions.text: When two or more clearly different phenotypes exist in the same population of species, the phenomenon is called?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Replication is an essential process because, whenever a cell divides, the two new daughter cells must contain the same genetic information, or DNA, as the parent cell. The replication process relies on the fact that each strand of DNA can serve as a template for duplication.

When two or more clearly different phenotypes exist in the same population of a species, the phenomenon is called "polymorphism." Polymorphism is a genetic variation within a population, where multiple distinct forms or phenotypes of a particular trait are observed. These different phenotypes are often the result of genetic differences or variations in the alleles of a gene. Polymorphism can be classified into different types based on the number of phenotypes present within a population: 1. Discontinuous or discrete polymorphism: In this type, distinct, non-overlapping phenotypes can be observed. An example of discontinuous polymorphism is the ABO blood group system in humans, where there are four different blood types (A, B, AB, and O), and individuals can have one of these specific blood types. 2. Continuous or quantitative polymorphism: In this type, the phenotypes form a continuum, and there are no distinct categories. Traits like height, weight, or skin color are examples of continuous polymorphism. Individuals in the population may display a range of values for these traits rather than being categorized into specific discrete groups. Polymorphism is a crucial aspect of genetic diversity within populations and can have various implications in terms of adaptation, evolution, and interactions with the environment.

Linkage, as related to genetics and genomics, refers to the closeness of genes or other DNA sequences to one another on the same chromosome. The closer two genes or sequences are to each other on a chromosome, the greater the probability that they will be inherited together.

Gene expression is the process by which the information encoded in a gene is turned into a function. This mostly occurs via the transcription of RNA molecules that code for proteins or non-coding RNA molecules that serve other functions.

questions.explanation: 'Polymorphism' means that different shapes and structures exist. 'A' is not the answer because replication is the division of the cell. 'C' is not the answer because Genetic linkage is the tendency of DNA sequences that are close together on a chromosome to be inherited together during the meiosis phase of sexual reproduction. 'D' is not the answer because Gene expression is the process by which information from a gene is used in the synthesis of a functional gene product that enables it to produce end products.

questions.text: Homozygous means:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is too vague. It mentions alleles but does not specify their combination or relation, which is crucial for defining 'homozygous'.

options.text: Two different alleles of a gene
options.explanation: Option B describes a heterozygous genotype, where two different alleles are present. This is the opposite of homozygous.
Option C incorrectly refers to identical genes rather than alleles. Homozygous specifically pertains to alleles, which are different forms of a single gene.

options.text: Having two identical alleles of a gene
options.explanation: Option D is correct. It accurately describes a homozygous genotype as having two identical alleles for a particular gene, whether those alleles are dominant or recessive.

questions.explanation: Homozygous refers to an organism having two identical alleles for a specific gene, meaning both alleles are the same, whether they are dominant or recessive. This is in contrast to heterozygous, where two different alleles are present. Option D correctly captures this definition. Option A is too general as it only mentions alleles without specifying their sameness or difference. Option B describes a heterozygous state, which involves different alleles. Option C incorrectly uses the term 'genes' instead of 'alleles', which is a crucial distinction when discussing genotypes.

questions.text: The phenomenon in which the effect of one allele in a heterozygous genotype completely masks the effect of another is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Codominance, as it relates to genetics, refers to a type of inheritance in which two versions (alleles) of the same gene are expressed separately to yield different traits in an individual.

Dominant refers to the relationship between two versions of a gene. Individuals receive two versions of each gene, known as alleles, from each parent. If the alleles of a gene are different, one allele will be expressed; it is the dominant gene. The effect of the other allele, called recessive, is masked.

Incomplete dominance is a form of Gene interaction in which both alleles of a gene at a locus are partially expressed, often resulting in an intermediate or different phenotype. It is also known as partial dominance.

Complete dominance is a form of dominance in the heterozygous condition wherein the allele that is regarded as dominant completely masks the effect of the allele that is recessive. For instance, for an individual carrying two alleles that are both dominant (e.g. AA), the trait that they represent will be expressed.

questions.explanation: In complete dominance, only one allele in the genotype is seen in the phenotype. In codominance, both alleles in the genotype are seen in the phenotype. In incomplete dominance, a mixture of the alleles in the genotype is seen in the phenotype.

questions.text: A family of five daughters is expecting another delivery. The chance of its beings a son is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The chances of a baby being either boy or girl are always 50% because in human beings there are 22 pairs of autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes. The female is homozygous while the male is heterozygous and genetically responsible for the sex of the child. Sperms are of two types, i.e. sperms have an X-chromosome which leads to a girl being born, and sperms have a Y-chromosome which is responsible for a boy being born. So, the correct answer is '50%'.

questions.text: Colour of flower indicates _ of the flower
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Genotype refers to the genetic constitution of living organisms.
A quality that sets one person or thing off from another
Phenotype refers to an individual's observable traits, such as height, eye color and blood type. A person's phenotype is determined by both their genomic makeup (genotype) and environmental factors.

questions.explanation: Phenotype refers to physical appearance of a trait. Flower colour is an example of phenotype like height, eye colour and skin colour etc.

questions.text: The following results of a cross between two individuals shown in the picture are: Which statement describes the children of the cross?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: One child is homozygous dominant, while the other exhibits a dominant phenotype but has a parent with a recessive phenotype.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the genetic cross shown does not allow for the production of a homozygous dominant child. The parents lack the necessary alleles for such an outcome.

options.text: Two child is homozygous recessive, and the other exhibits a dominant phenotype but has a parent with a recessive phenotype.
options.explanation: This option is correct. The cross results in one child inheriting recessive alleles from both parents, resulting in a homozygous recessive phenotype. The other child inherits a dominant allele from one parent and a recessive allele from the other, displaying a dominant phenotype while one parent exhibits a recessive phenotype.

options.text: One child is homozygous recessive, and the other exhibits a dominant phenotype but has a sibling with a recessive phenotype.
options.explanation: This option is misleading because the mention of a sibling does not provide meaningful genetic information about the relationship between the offspring, and the question does not specify sibling dynamics.

options.text: One child is homozygous recessive, and another exhibits a recessive phenotype, but has a parent with a dominant phenotype.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it incorrectly implies that both children have recessive phenotypes, which contradicts the presence of a dominant phenotype in one of the offspring.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B. In a Mendelian cross involving one parent who is homozygous recessive and another who is heterozygous, one offspring can be homozygous recessive, receiving recessive alleles from both parents, resulting in a recessive phenotype. The other offspring can be heterozygous, inheriting a dominant allele from one parent and a recessive allele from the other, which results in a dominant phenotype. Options A and D are incorrect due to incorrect assumptions about the potential genotypes of the offspring. Option C is misleading as it introduces unnecessary sibling relationships that do not contribute to solving the question.

questions.text: Hemophilia is a sex-linked _ trait.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hemophilia is not a dominant trait because it doesn't manifest in individuals who have only one copy of the allele. Instead, it requires two copies in females to manifest, or one in males due to their single X chromosome.
Codominance means both alleles are expressed equally in the phenotype, which is not the case with hemophilia. Hemophilia follows a recessive pattern of inheritance.
Pleiotropy refers to one gene affecting multiple traits, which is not the same concept as the recessive inheritance pattern seen in hemophilia.

Hemophilia is a sex-linked recessive trait, meaning the abnormal gene is carried on the X chromosome. Males, having only one X chromosome, are more likely to express the disorder if they have the recessive gene.

questions.explanation: Hemophilia is a sex-linked recessive disorder because the gene responsible is located on the X chromosome. In males (XY), the presence of the recessive allele on the X chromosome results in the disorder since there is no corresponding allele on the Y chromosome. In females (XX), a single recessive allele does not cause the disorder because the normal allele on the other X chromosome compensates. Therefore, females need two copies of the recessive allele to express the disorder. The other options are incorrect because they misrepresent the nature of hemophilia's inheritance. It is not dominant, as it does not appear in heterozygous females, nor is it codominant, as both alleles are not equally expressed. It is also not pleiotropic, which involves a single gene influencing multiple traits.

questions.text: A man who has type AB blood cannot be the father of a child with type _ blood, because he would pass on either the _ or the B allele to all of his offspring.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A man with type AB blood can have a child with type A blood if the mother provides an O allele. However, he cannot have a child with type O blood, as he will always pass on either an A or B allele.

A man with type AB blood cannot father a child with type O blood, as he will pass on either an A or B allele. Type O requires two O alleles, one from each parent. However, he can have a child with type A blood if the mother provides an O allele.
A man with type AB blood can have a child with type B blood if the mother provides an O allele. However, he cannot have a child with type O blood, as he will always pass on either an A or B allele.

A man with type AB blood can have a child with type B or A blood if the mother provides an O allele. Both are possible, but he still cannot have a child with type O blood, as he will always pass on either an A or B allele.

questions.explanation: A man with type AB blood possesses both A and B alleles. When he fathers a child, he will pass on either the A or B allele to his offspring. For a child to have type O blood, they must inherit an O allele from both parents. Since the man with type AB blood cannot provide an O allele, it is impossible for him to father a child with type O blood. This is why option B is correct. Other options such as type A and type B are possible if the mother contributes an O allele, as A and B are dominant over O.

questions.text: Crossing over brings:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Is correct as In genetics, the term "crossing over" refers to the exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes that occurs during meiosis. This process plays an important role in generating genetic diversity, as it can create new combinations of alleles on each chromosome. During crossing over, the chromatids of two homologous chromosomes pair up and exchange corresponding segments of DNA. This results in a mixing of genetic material, as some of the alleles on each chromosome are replaced with new alleles from the other chromosome.

This is incorrect as evolution brings new and better traits in individuals not crossing over In genetics, the term "crossing over" refers to the exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes that occurs during meiosis. This process plays an important role in generating genetic diversity, as it can create new combinations of alleles on each chromosome.
This is incorrect as Meiosis is a type of cell division that reduces the number of chromosomes in the parent cell by half and produces four gamete cells.
This is incorrect as the segregation of genes occurs in meiosis.

This results in a mixing of genetic material, as some of the alleles on each chromosome are replaced with new alleles from the other chromosome.
